---
title: "Opportunism detect and stats 2022G1"
author: "Xavier Francis"
date: "2026-04-01"
output: html_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

#Load packages
```{r}
library(readr)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(data.table) 
library(ggplot2)
library(chemodiv)
library(introdataviz)
library(lme4)
library(multcomp)
library(sjPlot)
library(emdist)
library(purrr)
library(EloRating)
library(tibble)
library(here)

```





#Data cleaning chunk 1. Here and in the next several data cleaning chunks we are getting rid of duplicate observations, unknown behaviors, and dealing with undirected behaviors
``` {r}
#group 1

matrix.please<-function(x) {
  m<-as.matrix(x[,-1])
  rownames(m)<-x[,1]
  m
}

#load data
`2022_birdIDsg1` <- read_csv(here("ch1 data","2022_birdIDs.csv"))


`2022_interactions_rawg1` <- read_csv(here("ch1 data","2022_interactions_group1.csv"))
raw.allbehavedata <- `2022_interactions_rawg1`


birdIDs_capture <- read_csv(here("ch1 data","2022_birdIDs.csv"))

birdIDs_capture <- read_csv(here("ch1 data","2022_birdIDs.csv")) 
birdIDs <- birdIDs_capture %>%
  dplyr::select(mark_id22) %>%
  filter(mark_id22!="") # all 20 birds





bird.list <- sort(unique(birdIDs$mark_id22)) # ensure that correct column is selected
length(bird.list)
bird.list
length(bird.list)
# list of all possible dyads
dyad.list <- expand.grid(bird.list, bird.list) #head(dyad.list)
names(dyad.list) <- c("actor", "subject")
dyad.list <- subset(dyad.list, actor!=subject)
dyad.list$dyadID <- paste(dyad.list$actor, dyad.list$subject, sep="-")
# list of days with observations
obsday.list <- as.character(unique(raw.allbehavedata$date)) #only days with observations
obsday.list.wday <- as.data.frame(obsday.list)
colnames(obsday.list.wday) <- "date"
obsday.list.wday$nday <- seq(1:length(obsday.list.wday$date))



```

#Data cleaning chunk 2; "other" and "unknown" behaviors
```{r}
behave<-raw.allbehavedata 
unique(behave$behavior)
# other
behave.other<-filter(behave, behavior=="other") %>% dplyr::select(-behavior) %>%
  rename(behavior=other) %>%
  dplyr::select(orderkey, sessionKEY, session_start_timeStamp, date, full_time, time, actor, subject, behavior, everything())
behave.exl.other<-filter(behave, behavior!="other") %>% 
  dplyr::select(-other)
behave.goodother<-bind_rows(behave.exl.other, behave.other)
# unknown
behave.unknown <- filter(behave.goodother, behavior=="unknown") 
behave.unknown$behavior[behave.unknown$behavior=="unknown"] <-"unk"
behave.unknown <- behave.unknown %>%
  unite(behavior, behavior, unknown, sep = "_")
behave$behavior[behave$behavior=="unk agonistic"] <-"unk_agonistic"
behave.exl.unk<-behave.goodother %>% filter(behavior!="unknown") %>%
  dplyr::select(-unknown)
behave.goodunk<-bind_rows(behave.unknown, behave.exl.unk)
behave<-behave.goodunk
head(behave)

unique(behave$actor)


```


#Data cleaning chunk 3; prepare cleaned df
```{r}
# keep only observations where the actor is in the list of bird IDs
behave.goodID <- subset(behave, actor %in% bird.list)
# keep only observations where the actor (found above) and the subject are both in the list of bird IDs
behave.goodID <- subset(behave.goodID, subject %in% bird.list)
#finds n behaviors by sessionKEY, date, time, actor, subject, behavior
behave.key <- behave.goodID %>% 
  dplyr::group_by(sessionKEY, date, full_time,actor, subject, behavior) %>% 
  tally() # counts behaviors that were observed within the same minute
#finds max n behaviors per actor by date, time, and behavior type. Return just that summary
behave.maxkey<- behave.key %>% 
  group_by(actor, subject, date, full_time, behavior) %>%
  slice(which.max(n)) %>% # n.aggXaggXkey if summarise, n if tally
  ungroup()
head(behave.maxkey)
#finds n disps per actor by date for summarized/trimmed data
behaveXday <- behave.maxkey %>% 
  group_by(actor, subject, date, full_time, behavior) %>% 
  summarise(n=sum(n))
head(behaveXday)
min(behaveXday$n)





unique(behaveXday$behavior)
# behaviors in long format (1 event at a time)
behaveXday.long <- behaveXday %>%
  uncount(n) %>%
  mutate(n=1)
sum(behaveXday.long$n)
sum(behaveXday$n)
head(behaveXday.long)
## DUPLICATE UNDIRECTED BEHAVIORS
# filter the undirected behaviors 
behaveXday.undirected <- behaveXday.long %>% 
  filter(grepl(pattern="each other",tolower(behavior)))
  
behaveXday.undirected1 <-behaveXday.long %>%
  filter( behavior=="s2s") # spar as well?
behaveXday.undirected <- bind_rows(behaveXday.undirected,behaveXday.undirected1 )
# duplicate undirected dataframe and change subject and actor
behaveXday.undirected.dupl <- behaveXday.undirected %>%
  rename(subject=actor, actor=subject) %>%
  dplyr::select(actor, subject, everything())
# bind both dataframes
behaveXday.directed2022G1.comb <- bind_rows(behaveXday.undirected, behaveXday.undirected.dupl) 
behaveXday.undir2dir <- behaveXday.directed2022G1.comb %>%
  separate( behavior,into = c("behavior", "direction"), sep = "_") %>%
  dplyr::select(-direction)
unique(behaveXday.undir2dir$behavior)
## COMBINE DATAFRAMES
# bind to directed dataframe
behaveXday.directed2022G1 <- behaveXday.long %>% 
  filter(!grepl(pattern="each other",tolower(behavior)) & behavior!="s2s")
unique(behaveXday.directed2022G1$behavior)
behaveXday.directed2022G1 <- bind_rows(behaveXday.directed2022G1, behaveXday.undir2dir)
behaveXday.directed2022G1$behavior[behaveXday.directed2022G1$behavior=="NN"] <- "nn"
behaveXday.directed2022G1$behavior[behaveXday.directed2022G1$behavior=="alloP"] <- "allop"
behaveXday.directed2022G1$behavior[behaveXday.directed2022G1$behavior=="allopreen"] <- "allop"
length(behaveXday.directed2022G1$actor)
unique(behaveXday.directed2022G1$behavior)

#view completed df
head(behaveXday.directed2022G1)

```


#prepare df (filter by behavior type and date and attach ranks)
```{r}


#filter out non aggression data
't2022_aggression_onlyG1' <- filter(behaveXday.directed2022G1, behavior %in% c("crowd","displace", "chase"))




#subset for time period of interest (Here we are subsetting for the first stable period after group formation)
t2022_aggression_only_readyG1 <- t2022_aggression_onlyG1 %>% 
  filter(date >= as.Date("2022-1-26"),
         date <= as.Date("2022-1-28"))





#attach ranks onto df
#load in the bird power score ranks
mopa_power_2022G1 <- read_csv(here("ch1 data","MOPA_power score_2022.csv"))
#subset for appropriate bin
mopa_power_2022G1 <- subset(mopa_power_2022G1, bin == 2)
#keep only rank and birdID
mopa_power_2022G1 <- mopa_power_2022G1 %>% 
  dplyr::select(id, rank)
# join rank to actor and subject on aggression df

unordered_both_numbered <- t2022_aggression_only_readyG1 %>%
  left_join(mopa_power_2022G1, by = c("actor" = "id")) %>%
  rename(actor_rank = rank) %>%              
  left_join(mopa_power_2022G1, by = c("subject" = "id")) %>%
  rename(subject_rank = rank)          


# order correctly and format into a date time column for the first column in the edgelist
unordered_both_numbered <- unordered_both_numbered %>% dplyr::select(full_time, everything())

#order df correctly by time and date
both_numbered_sorted2022G1 <- unordered_both_numbered %>%
  arrange(unordered_both_numbered$date, unordered_both_numbered$full_time)


#ensure that rank is numeric
both_numbered_sorted2022G1$actor_rank <- as.numeric(both_numbered_sorted2022G1$actor_rank)
both_numbered_sorted2022G1$subject_rank <- as.numeric(both_numbered_sorted2022G1$subject_rank)

head(both_numbered_sorted2022G1)


both_numbered_sorted2022G1$actor
length(both_numbered_sorted2022G1$full_time)
```




#Attach observation session to df so we can permute within observation sessions
```{r}
#start with both_numbered_sorted2022G1
both_numbered_sorted2022G1 <- ungroup(both_numbered_sorted2022G1)

#double check that ranks are numeric 
both_numbered_sorted2022G1$actor_rank <- as.numeric(both_numbered_sorted2022G1$actor_rank)
both_numbered_sorted2022G1$subject_rank <- as.numeric(both_numbered_sorted2022G1$subject_rank)

head(both_numbered_sorted2022G1)


#procedure to add obs session

#store the first row as a separate df. It will be binded back on later 
row_1_obs <- both_numbered_sorted2022G1[1,]

#put events side by side. This will allow us to subtract consecutive times from one another to determine whether an event took place on a separate observation session. 
obs_period_pt_1 <- mutate(both_numbered_sorted2022G1, actor_2 = lead(actor),
actor_2_rank = lead(actor_rank), 
subject_2 = lead(subject),                                   
subject_2_rank = lead(subject_rank), 
 full_time_2 = lead(full_time), 
date2 = lead(date))

#add empty columns for observation period and date period
obs_period_pt_1$obs_period <- NA
obs_period_pt_1$date_period <- NA

#create ticker for loop
q <- 1

#loop to calculate observation sessions. If consecutive events were on the same day but there was a long time between events, it is determined that the 2nd event took place in a separate observation session. The ticker goes up, and is reset if events took place on different days.
for (i in 1:nrow(obs_period_pt_1)) {ifelse(obs_period_pt_1$date[i] == obs_period_pt_1$date2[i]
                                            & obs_period_pt_1$full_time_2[i] - obs_period_pt_1$full_time[i] >= 3000, q <- q+1, q <- q)
  ifelse(obs_period_pt_1$date[i] != obs_period_pt_1$date2[i], q <- 1, q <- q)
  obs_period_pt_1$obs_period [i] <- q
  }

#remove original columns and keep mutated columns. These will replace original columns
obs_period_pt_2 <- subset(obs_period_pt_1, select = c(date2,  full_time_2, actor_2, actor_2_rank, subject_2, subject_2_rank, obs_period, date_period  ))

#get rid of the bottom, which will be full of NAs, as the last event in the original data does not have anything that comes after
obs_period_pt_3 <- slice(obs_period_pt_2, 1:(n() - 1))

#rename mutated columns with original names 
setnames(obs_period_pt_3, old = c('date2',  'full_time_2', 'actor_2', 'actor_2_rank', 'subject_2', 'subject_2_rank'), 
         new = c('date',  'full_time', 'actor', 'actor_rank', 'subject', 'subject_rank'))

#add observation period "1" onto first row that we subseted off, as the first event in the data has to take place in obs session 1
row_1_obs$obs_period <- 1 
row_1_obs$date_period <- NA

#ensure that row 1 matches the df
row_1_obs_ready <-  subset(row_1_obs, select = c(date,  full_time, actor, actor_rank, subject, subject_rank, obs_period, date_period  ))

#bind first row and df together
obs_period_pt_4 <- rbind(row_1_obs_ready, obs_period_pt_3)
#add date period 
obs_period_pt_4$date_period <- paste(obs_period_pt_4$date,obs_period_pt_4$obs_period)


#arrange by date and time
obs_period_pt_4 <- arrange(obs_period_pt_4, date, full_time)



head(obs_period_pt_4)
```

#Separate main df into a list of dfs. Each df contains data for each respective bird in each respective observation session 
```{r}
head(obs_period_pt_4)



#Prepare/order and give each row an id and numeric time
df <- obs_period_pt_4 %>%
  arrange(date_period, date, full_time) %>%
  mutate(.row = row_number(),
         tsec = as.numeric(full_time))  # seconds since midnight

#Create previous by actor columns. These are used to identify aggressive events that occur within 60 sec (may represent chase)  
by_actor <- df %>%
  group_by(date_period, actor) %>%
  mutate(prev_subj_by_actor = lag(subject),
         prev_tsec_by_actor = lag(tsec)) %>%
  ungroup()

#Last involvement time for each bird (actor or subject) 
#Build a long view with one entry per participant per event
long <- bind_rows(
  by_actor %>% transmute(date_period, .row, participant = actor, tsec),
  by_actor %>% transmute(date_period, .row, participant = subject, tsec)
) %>%
  arrange(date_period, tsec, .row) %>%
  group_by(date_period, participant) %>%
  mutate(prev_involve_tsec = lag(tsec)) %>%
  ungroup()

#For each original row get the most recent involvement of either bird A or bird B before this row
last_involve <- long %>%
  group_by(date_period, .row) %>%
  summarise(last_involve_tsec = max(prev_involve_tsec, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  mutate(last_involve_tsec = ifelse(is.finite(last_involve_tsec), last_involve_tsec, NA_real_))

#Apply rule of getting rid of consecutive 60 sec possible chases
aggression_no_repeat_custom <- by_actor %>%
  left_join(last_involve, by = c("date_period", ".row")) %>%
  mutate(
    #Base repeat condition. check if previous by same actor is the same subject and within 60s
    within_actor_repeat = !is.na(prev_tsec_by_actor) &
                          subject == prev_subj_by_actor &
                          (tsec - prev_tsec_by_actor) <= 60,

    #Check was there an intervening event involving bird A or bird B after previous A to B
    interrupted_by_A_or_B = within_actor_repeat &
                            !is.na(last_involve_tsec) &
                            (last_involve_tsec > prev_tsec_by_actor),

    keep = !within_actor_repeat | interrupted_by_A_or_B
  ) %>%
  filter(keep) %>%
dplyr::select(-.row, -tsec,
         -prev_subj_by_actor, -prev_tsec_by_actor,
         -last_involve_tsec, -within_actor_repeat, -interrupted_by_A_or_B, -keep)


obs <- aggression_no_repeat_custom$date_period
#separate data into a list with each df=a date 


#create empty list to store each bird each day each obs
opp_aggression_list_2022G1 <- list()
actor <- unique(t2022_aggression_onlyG1$actor)

dater <- unique(obs)
datex <- c(1: length(unique(obs)))
actorer <- actor
actex <- c(1:length(actor))




#separate data into list of each obs session each day for each bird
for(i in datex) { 
  for (g in actex){
  output <- filter(aggression_no_repeat_custom, aggression_no_repeat_custom$date_period == dater[i] & (subject == actorer[g] | actor == actorer[g]))
   if (nrow(output) < 1) {output [ nrow(output) + 1 , ] <- NA
    } 
  output <- mutate(output, counter = actorer[g])
      opp_aggression_list_2022G1[[paste0("list", dater[i], actorer[g])]] <- output}}
```

#Put events side by side
```{r}

#arrange df by date and time
opp_aggression_list_2022G1 <- lapply(opp_aggression_list_2022G1, function(x) arrange(x, date, full_time))

#put df side by side
opp_side_by_side_time <- lapply(opp_aggression_list_2022G1, function(x) mutate(x, actor_2 = lead(actor), 
 actor_2_rank = lead(actor_rank), subject_2 = lead(subject),  subject_2_rank = lead(subject_rank),  full_time_2 = lead(full_time),  date2 = lead(date)))


```


#Response Motif Speed
```{r}
#######################################################################################################

#follows similar procedure as calculating the count of response motif execution events for a respective rule , with some differences in the rule detection loop and the structure

#counter identifies the focal individual for each dataframe after the
#master interaction dataframe is split into individual level dfs.
#Each df contains all events involving that individual (in any role).
#Using counter ensures that sequential behvaioral sequences are evaluated from the
#correct individual’s perspective, reducing false positives that could
#occur if a pattern appears in one df but is actually explained by
#interactions only visible in another individual’s df.

#For example in individual A’s df we might see A attack B, then B attacks A, which appears to be retaliation. However, A’s df only includes interactions involving A and cannot show
# whether B interacted with others in between the initiating event and the aggressive response.
#If we look at B’s df (counter == B), we might see that the full sequence is A defeats B, then B defeats C, then B defeats A. Because B interacted with C before responding to A, the sequence was
#interrupted and is not counted as the response motif for retaliation. This interruption is invisible in A’s df and would create a false positive, which is why rules must be evaluated in the correct
# counter (focal individual) dataframe.

# In this loop initiated marks whether the focal individual was the subject. rule_followed indicates that the behavioral and time constraint patterns associated with a certain rule's response motif were followed and that the focal individual (counter) is correctly involved








#test for length of pileon in seconds
opp_side_by_side_tested_time <-  map(opp_side_by_side_time, ~ mutate(.x, 
                                initiated = ifelse(subject == counter 
                                                   & subject_2 == counter
                                                   & subject_2_rank < actor_2_rank
                                                   & actor != actor_2
                                                   & subject == subject_2, "yes","no"), 
                                rule_followed = ifelse( subject_2_rank < actor_2_rank
                                                   & subject == counter
                                                   & subject_2 == subject
                                                   & actor != actor_2
                                                   & subject_2 == counter
                                                   & full_time_2 - full_time > 0
                                                   & date2 == date, "yes","no"),
                                time_to_opp = full_time_2 - full_time ))


#bind list of dfs into one df
opp_huge_time <- bind_rows(opp_side_by_side_tested_time, .id = "column_label")

#sort by time and date
 opp_huge_sorted_time <- opp_huge_time %>%
  arrange(opp_huge_time$date, opp_huge_time$full_time)

 
#remove any duplicate or double counted events 
opp_huge_sorted_no_dupr_time <- subset(opp_huge_sorted_time, initiated != "no") 
 
 #subset for columns of interest
 opp_no_dupr_time <- subset(opp_huge_sorted_no_dupr_time, select = c(date, full_time, actor, actor_rank, subject, subject_rank, counter, actor_2, actor_2_rank, subject_2, subject_2_rank, full_time_2, date2,initiated, rule_followed, time_to_opp, date_period))
 
 
#get df where motif was executed
opp_length <- subset(opp_no_dupr_time, rule_followed != "no")

#subset for columns of interest
opp_length_ready_2022G1 <- subset( opp_length, select = c(time_to_opp, actor, subject, actor_2, subject_2, date_period))


#obtain value for number of times motif was executed
opp <- length(opp_length_ready_2022G1$time_to_opp)


#summarize the speed of motif execution Here we are checking whether the speed of the motifs in question tends to be clustered
summary.opp <- opp_length_ready_2022G1 %>% group_by(time_to_opp) %>%
  dplyr::summarize (
    count =n())


summary.opp


mean(opp_length_ready_2022G1$time_to_opp)

#quick glimpses of the distribution of motif speeds
ggplot(opp_length_ready_2022G1, aes(x=time_to_opp)) + 
  geom_histogram(aes(y=..density..),      
                 binwidth=10,
                 colour="black", fill="white") + 
  geom_density() +   scale_x_continuous(name = "length in s of opp along events", limits = c(0,3000)) 


```

#Randomize Motif speed
```{r}

#randomize time between initiation and response between events
#ALWAYS RUN OBSERVED TIME CODE FIR
obs <- aggression_no_repeat_custom$date_period





#create empty list to store each bird each day each obs
practdrlist_ordered <- list()

dater <- unique(obs)
datex <- c(1: length(unique(obs)))
actorer <- actor
actex <- c(1:length(actor))


#separate data into list of each obs session each day for each bird
for(i in datex) { 
  for (g in actex){
  output <- filter(aggression_no_repeat_custom, aggression_no_repeat_custom$date_period == dater[i] & (subject == actorer[g] | actor == actorer[g]))
   if (nrow(output) < 1) {output [ nrow(output) + 1 , ] <- NA
    } 
  output <- mutate(output, counter = actorer[g])
      practdrlist_ordered[[paste0("list", dater[i], actorer[g])]] <- output}}








#arrange df by date and time
practdrlist_ordered <- lapply(practdrlist_ordered, function(x) arrange(x, date, full_time))



#randomize time between initiation and response between events
opp_side_by_side <- lapply(practdrlist_ordered, function(x) mutate(x, actor_2 = lead(actor), actor_2_rank = lead(actor_rank), subject_2 = lead(subject), subject_2_rank = lead(subject_rank), full_time_2 = lead(full_time), date2 = lead(date))) 

#Loop to detect instances and speed of rule use for the rule in question 
opp_side_by_side_tested_time <-  map(opp_side_by_side, ~ mutate(.x, initiated = ifelse(
  subject == counter
  & subject_2 == counter
  & subject_2_rank < actor_2_rank 
  & actor != actor_2
  & subject == subject_2, "yes","no"),
rule_followed = ifelse( subject_2_rank < actor_2_rank
  & subject == counter
  & subject_2 == subject
  & actor != actor_2
  & subject_2 == counter
  & full_time_2 - full_time > 0
  & date2 == date, "yes","no"),
time_to_opp = full_time_2 - full_time ))
                                                               

#combine dataframes into one huge dataframe
opp_huge_time <- bind_rows(opp_side_by_side_tested_time, .id = "column_label")






#Remove duplicate and double counted sequences
opp_huge_time <- distinct(opp_huge_time, date, full_time, actor, actor_rank, subject, subject_rank, obs_period, date_period, actor_2, subject_2, full_time_2, date2, time_to_opp, .keep_all= TRUE) 



#remove instances of motif execution in which the time between events was calculated to be 0. In this case we cant be certain of which event actually came first
opp_huge_time <- filter(opp_huge_time, time_to_opp != 0)


date_x <- opp_huge_time$date
obs <- opp_huge_time$date_period
#separate data into a list with each df=a date so we can randomize within days


rule_time_ready_for_rand_list <- list()
dater <- unique(obs)
datex <- c(1: length(unique(obs)))

#loop to create a separate dataframe for of each obs session each day 
#Each dataframe is stored within a list to make the dataframes easier to work with
#We conserve our permutation randomizations later within days and, more specifically, within observation sessions 
for(i in datex) { 
  output <- filter(opp_huge_time, opp_huge_time$date_period == dater[i] )
   if (nrow(output) < 1) {output [ nrow(output) + 1 , ] <- NA
    } 
      rule_time_ready_for_rand_list[[paste0("list", dater[i])]] <- output}


nruns <- 1000



pb = txtProgressBar(min=0, max = nruns, style = 3)

#run ids should be 1 through however many times you want to run through the permutation
runIDs <- seq(1,1000)

#create an empty masterframe to be filled with each run of the permutation
masterframe_opp.df_time_2022G1 <- data.frame(runIDs = character(),
                             actor = character(),
                             subject = character(),
                             date = character(),
                             full_time = integer(),
                             counter = character(),
                             actor_2 = character(),
                             subject_2 = character(),
                             full_time_2 = integer(),
                             date2 = character(),
                             initiated = character(),
                             rule_followed = character(),
                             time_to_opp = integer(),
                             date_period = character(),
                             stringsAsFactors=FALSE)


set.seed(777)
#forloop starts here'
for (r in 1:length(runIDs)) { #r = 1 is a test you can uncomment to see if it works before #running it 1000 times
#r = 1
  run <- r
      setTxtProgressBar(pb, r) #update progress bar
  loop.run <- paste("run", run) 
  loop.run
  #for example, if r = 1, looprun will say run 1 which will allow you to keep each run's #data straight
  
    

  
  #renamed data
  loop.datalist <- rule_time_ready_for_rand_list
  head(loop.datalist[[3]])
  xorder <- c()
  listlength <- as.numeric(length(loop.datalist))
datex2 <- 1:listlength
  full_time_to_rule <-c()
  loop.data.randomized.time_to_rule.added <- c()
    
    

  for(i in datex2) {
         #store time
     full_time_to_rule[[i]] <- subset(loop.datalist[[i]], select = c(time_to_opp))     
     #remove time from each df in the list. randomizing the order of time wouldnt make sense
     loop.datalist[[i]] <- subset(loop.datalist[[i]], select = -c(time_to_opp))
     #create a randomized sequence of numbers to bind to the subset data (ex. 2,6,1,3,5,4)
      #sort the data frame to put the randomized numbers in order from 1 to the max which results in the time between events (speed) has been  randomized (ex. from above 1,2,3,4,5,6)
    xorder[[i]] <- data.frame(xorder = sample(1:length(full_time_to_rule[[i]]$time_to_opp),
                                              length(full_time_to_rule[[i]]$time_to_opp)))
    #bind random order to df
    full_time_to_rule[[i]] <- mutate(full_time_to_rule[[i]], 
                                 xorderx = xorder[[i]]$xorder)
    #put randomized order of numbers in order to randomize speed in the dataset
    full_time_to_rule[[i]] <-
      full_time_to_rule[[i]][order(full_time_to_rule[[i]]$xorderx),]
    #bind time back on
    loop.data.randomized.time_to_rule.added[[i]] <- bind_cols(loop.datalist[[i]], full_time_to_rule[[i]])
    loop.data.randomized.time_to_rule.added[[i]] <- subset(loop.data.randomized.time_to_rule.added[[i]], select = -c(xorderx))

   }
  

  
  
  
  
  
  
  

#combine dataframes into one huge dataframe
loop.datalist_time_randomized_binded_tested_opp_2022G1 <- bind_rows(loop.data.randomized.time_to_rule.added, .id = "column_label")


 

#sort by time and date
loop.datalist_time_randomized_binded_tested_opp_2022G1 <- loop.datalist_time_randomized_binded_tested_opp_2022G1 %>%
  arrange(loop.datalist_time_randomized_binded_tested_opp_2022G1$date, loop.datalist_time_randomized_binded_tested_opp_2022G1$full_time)






#Remove duplicate events, events that have been represented twice, and events that could not confidently be counted as the start of the opportunism response motif
testhugesorted_no_dup_rand_time <- subset(loop.datalist_time_randomized_binded_tested_opp_2022G1, initiated != "no") 

opp_no_dup_rand_time <- subset(testhugesorted_no_dup_rand_time, select = c(date, full_time, actor, subject, counter, actor_2, subject_2, full_time_2, date2,initiated,  rule_followed,time_to_opp, date_period))





  
  #end of opp process
  #end of rule code
  
  
  
#bind vector of run IDs for each run to the dataframe to have a numeric run id next to each data point that corresponds with that run which will allow us to sort them in ascending order later
 runIDs <- rep(loop.run, length(opp_no_dup_rand_time$actor))
 
  opp_complete_rand_time_2022G1 <- cbind(runIDs, opp_no_dup_rand_time)
  
  
#put each runs data into the masterframe
  masterframe_opp.df_time_2022G1 <- rbind(masterframe_opp.df_time_2022G1, opp_complete_rand_time_2022G1)
  
}

#write.csv(masterframe_opp.df_time_2022G1,  here("ch1 data","opp2022G1timemaster.csv"), row.names = FALSE)
```

#Compare observed speed to random
```{r}

#masterframe_opp.df_time_2022G1 <- read_csv(here("ch1 data","opp2022G1timemaster.csv"))


head(masterframe_opp.df_time_2022G1)

#subset masterframe so that only motifs associated with rules are included
masterframe_opp.df_time_2022G1_only_follows <- subset(masterframe_opp.df_time_2022G1, rule_followed == "yes" )

#isolate time it takes to execute a motif
masterframe_opp_time_sub.df_2022G1 <- subset( masterframe_opp.df_time_2022G1_only_follows, select = c(actor, subject, actor_2,subject_2, time_to_opp,date_period))
head(masterframe_opp_time_sub.df_2022G1)



#add data label for the randomized motif speed
masterframe_opp_time_ready.df <- mutate(masterframe_opp_time_sub.df_2022G1, data_type = "randomized")

#ensure that time is numeric
masterframe_opp_time_ready.df$time_to_opp <- as.numeric(masterframe_opp_time_ready.df$time_to_opp)


head(opp_length_ready_2022G1)

#add data label for the observed motif speed
opp_length_ready_2022G1_label <- mutate(opp_length_ready_2022G1, data_type = "observed")


#combine df for observed motif speed and randomized motif speed into one df
opp_length_ready_2022G1_comb <- rbind(masterframe_opp_time_ready.df, opp_length_ready_2022G1_label )
unique(opp_length_ready_2022G1_comb$data_type)




#provide label for motif in question
opp_length_ready_2022G1_comb <- mutate(opp_length_ready_2022G1_comb, rule = "opportunism")


#make sure that motif speed is numeric
opp_length_ready_2022G1_comb$time_to_opp <- as.numeric(opp_length_ready_2022G1_comb$time_to_opp)


#split violin plot of observed vs randomized motif speed
opp_violin_plot <- ggplot(opp_length_ready_2022G1_comb, aes(rule, time_to_opp, fill = data_type))  +
  introdataviz::geom_split_violin(alpha= .9, trim = TRUE, show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_boxplot(width = .2, alpha = .2, fatten = 2, show.legend = FALSE, outlier.shape = NA) +
  scale_x_discrete(name = "Time Dependent Rule") +  
     coord_cartesian(ylim = c(0, 1000)) +
  scale_y_continuous(name = "Speed of opportunism events (s)",
                     breaks = seq(0, 1000, 200) 
                     ) + scale_fill_manual(values=c("red","gray")) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 20)  + theme(axis.text.y = element_text(size=25), axis.title= element_blank(), axis.text.x = element_blank())


opp_2022G1_time_plot <- opp_violin_plot

opp_2022G1_time_plot

#calculate the means for observed and randomized motif speed
mean_obs_rand_opportunism_2022G1 <- opp_length_ready_2022G1_comb %>% group_by(data_type) %>%
  dplyr::summarize (mean(time_to_opp))

mean_obs_rand_opportunism_2022G1







median_obs_rand_opp_2022G1 <- opp_length_ready_2022G1_comb %>% group_by(data_type) %>%
  dplyr::summarize (median(time_to_opp))

median_obs_rand_opp_2022G1









m1_opp_2022G1 <- glmer(time_to_opp ~ data_type  + (1 | date_period) , data = opp_length_ready_2022G1_comb ,   control = glmerControl(optimizer = "bobyqa", optCtrl = list(maxfun=2e5)), family = Gamma(link = "log"))
summary(m1_opp_2022G1)




confint_opp_2022G1 <- round(confint(m1_opp_2022G1, method = "Wald", level = 0.95), 3)
confint_opp_2022G1

#Tukey
summary(glht(m1_opp_2022G1, linfct = mcp(data_type="Tukey")), test = adjusted("none"))

#r2 values
library(MuMIn)
r.squaredGLMM(m1_opp_2022G1 )


```

#Earth Movers Distance Calculation
```{r}
#ensure that observed motif speed is numeric
opp_length_ready_2022G1$time_to_opp <- as.numeric(opp_length_ready_2022G1$time_to_opp)


head(opp_length_ready_2022G1)

#convert to a density matrix
#summarize the count of various motif speeds

summary.opp_length_ready_2022G1 <- opp_length_ready_2022G1 %>% group_by(time_to_opp) %>%
  dplyr::summarize (
    count =n())

summary.opp_length_ready_2022G1
#Add density column
summary.opp_length_ready_2022G1_density <- mutate(summary.opp_length_ready_2022G1, density = count/sum(count))
#remove count column
summary.opp_length_ready_2022G1_density_no_count <- subset(summary.opp_length_ready_2022G1_density, select = -c(count))
#reorder the columns
summary.opp_length_ready_2022G1_density_no_count_reordered <- summary.opp_length_ready_2022G1_density_no_count[, c(2, 1)]
head(summary.opp_length_ready_2022G1_density_no_count_reordered)
#convert to matrix
summary.opp_length_ready_2022G1_density_no_count_reordered_mat <- as.matrix(summary.opp_length_ready_2022G1_density_no_count_reordered)





masterframe_length_ready <- subset(masterframe_opp_time_sub.df_2022G1, select = c(time_to_opp))
head(masterframe_length_ready)



#ensure that observed motif speed is numeric
masterframe_length_ready$time_to_opp <- as.numeric(masterframe_length_ready$time_to_opp)



#convert to a density matrix
#summarize the count of various motif speeds

summary.masterframe_length_ready <- masterframe_length_ready %>% group_by(time_to_opp) %>%
  dplyr::summarize (
    count =n())

summary.masterframe_length_ready

#Adddensity column
summary.masterframe_length_ready_density <- mutate(summary.masterframe_length_ready, density = count/sum(count))
#remove count column
summary.masterframe_length_ready_density_no_count <- subset(summary.masterframe_length_ready_density, select = -c(count))
#reorder the columns
summary.masterframe_length_ready_density_no_count_reordered <- summary.masterframe_length_ready_density_no_count[, c(2, 1)]
head(summary.masterframe_length_ready_density_no_count_reordered)
#convert to matrix
summary.masterframe_length_ready_density_no_count_reordered_mat <- as.matrix(summary.masterframe_length_ready_density_no_count_reordered)


#Calculate EMD between observed and randomized motif speed
opp_obs_vs_rand_emd <- emdist::emd(summary.opp_length_ready_2022G1_density_no_count_reordered_mat, summary.masterframe_length_ready_density_no_count_reordered_mat)
opp_obs_vs_rand_emd
#284.7199
```

#Calculate Observed Opportunism (interval = 4 thresholds)
```{r}
#put df side by side
opp_side_by_side <- lapply(opp_aggression_list_2022G1, function(x) mutate(x, actor_2 = lead(actor), 
 actor_2_rank = lead(actor_rank), subject_2 = lead(subject),  subject_2_rank = lead(subject_rank),  full_time_2 = lead(full_time),  date2 = lead(date)))


#counter identifies the focal individual for each dataframe after the
#master interaction dataframe is split into individual level dfs.
#Each df contains all events involving that individual (in any role).
#Using counter ensures that sequential behvaioral sequences are evaluated from the
#correct individual’s perspective, reducing false positives that could
#occur if a pattern appears in one df but is actually explained by
#interactions only visible in another individual’s df.

#For example in individual A’s df we might see A attack B, then B attacks A, which appears to be retaliation. However, A’s df only includes interactions involving A and cannot show
# whether B interacted with others in between the initiating event and the aggressive response.
#If we look at B’s df (counter == B), we might see that the full sequence is A defeats B, then B defeats C, then B defeats A. Because B interacted with C before responding to A, the sequence was
#interrupted and is not counted as the response motif for retaliation. This interruption is invisible in A’s df and would create a false positive, which is why rules must be evaluated in the correct
# counter (focal individual) dataframe.

# In this loop initiated marks whether the focal individual was the subject. rule_followed indicates that the behavioral and time constraint patterns associated with a certain rule's response motif were followed and that the focal individual (counter) is correctly involved


opp_side_by_side_tested <- map(opp_side_by_side, ~ mutate(.x, initiated = ifelse(
  subject == counter
  & subject_2 == counter
  & subject_2_rank < actor_2_rank 
  & actor != actor_2 & subject == subject_2, "yes","no"), 
  rule_followed = ifelse( 
    subject_2_rank < actor_2_rank 
  & subject == counter 
  & subject_2 == subject 
  & actor != actor_2
  & subject_2 == counter
  & full_time_2 - full_time <= 2444 
  & full_time_2 - full_time > 0
  & date2 == date,
"yes","no")))

#combine dataframes into one huge dataframe
opp_huge <- bind_rows(opp_side_by_side_tested, .id = "column_label")

#sort by time and date
opp_huge_sorted <- opp_huge %>%
  arrange(opp_huge$date, opp_huge$full_time)

#Remove duplicate events, events that have been represented twice, and events that can not be confidently counted as the start of the opp-along response motif
opp_huge_sorted_no_dupr <- subset(opp_huge_sorted, initiated != "no") 


#subset for desired columns
opp_no_dupr <- subset(opp_huge_sorted_no_dupr, select = c(date, full_time, actor, actor_rank, subject, subject_rank, 
                                                                        counter, actor_2, actor_2_rank, subject_2, subject_2_rank, full_time_2, date2, 
                                                                        initiated, rule_followed))

table(opp_no_dupr$rule_followed)

#determine how many time response motif was executed
opp_real_2022G1 <- length(which(opp_no_dupr$rule_followed == "yes"))


opp_real_2022G1_frame <- data.frame(opp_real_2022G1)





#create df of only response motifs
opp_doner_all_yes <-filter(opp_no_dupr, rule_followed == "yes")


opp_real_2022G1_frame
```


#Randomize/permute order of events
```{r}
#################################################loop#################################################################

#start with aggression_no_repeat_custom
head(aggression_no_repeat_custom)


obs <- aggression_no_repeat_custom$date_period
#separate data into a list with each df=a date period so we can randomize within days and obs periods

datelist <- list()
dater <- unique(obs)
datex <- c(1: length(unique(obs)))

#loop to create a separate dataframe for of each obs session each day 
#Each dataframe is stored within a list to make the dataframes easy to work with
#We wish to conserve our permutation randomization within days and, more specifically, within observation sessions
for(i in datex) { 
  output <- filter(aggression_no_repeat_custom, aggression_no_repeat_custom$date_period == dater[i] )
   if (nrow(output) < 1) {output [ nrow(output) + 1 , ] <- NA
    } 
      datelist[[paste0("list", dater[i])]] <- output}



#run ids should be 1 through however many times you want to run through the permutation. Here 1000 times
runIDs <- seq(1,1000)

#create an empty masterframe to be filled with each run of the permutation
masterframe_opp.df <- data.frame(runIDs = character(),
                                         actor = character(),
                                         subject = character(),
                                         date = character(),
                                         full_time = integer(),
                                         counter = character(),
                                         actor_2 = character(),
                                         subject_2 = character(),
                                         full_time_2 = integer(),
                                         date2 = character(),
                                         initiated = character(),
                                         rule_followed = character(),
                                         stringsAsFactors=FALSE)


nruns <- 1000
#add progress bar
pb <- txtProgressBar(min=0, max = nruns, style = 3)
#seed = 100 for 2444
#seed = 105 for 767
#seed = 111 for 1363
#seed = 45 for 414
#set seed
set.seed(100)

#forloop starts here'
for (r in 1:length(runIDs)) { #r = 1 is a test you can uncomment to see if it works before #running it 1000 times
  #r = 1
  run <- r
setTxtProgressBar(pb, r) #update progress bar

  loop.run <- paste("run", run) 
  loop.run
  #for example, if r = 1, looprun will say run 1 which will allow you to keep each run's data straight
  
  

  
   #renamed data
  loop.datalist <- datelist
  head(loop.datalist[[3]])
  xorder <- c()
  listlength <- as.numeric(length(loop.datalist))
  datex2 <- 1:listlength
  full_time_list <-c()
  loop.data.randomized.time.added <- c()
  #get rid of anything thats not actor and receiver (we will bind back on the date and time #later anyways to preserve
  #their order)
  


   for(i in datex2) {
     #store time
     full_time_list[[i]] <- subset(loop.datalist[[i]], select = c(full_time))
     #remove time from each df in the list. randomizing the order of time wouldnt make sense
     loop.datalist[[i]] <- subset(loop.datalist[[i]], select = -c(full_time))

      #create a randomized sequence of numbers to bind to the subset data (ex. 2,6,1,3,5,4)
      #sort the data frame to put the randomized numbers in order from 1 to whatever and voila, the order of events has been randomized (ex.  from above 1,2,3,4,5,6)
    xorder[[i]] <- data.frame(xorder = sample(1:length(loop.datalist[[i]]$actor),
                                              length(loop.datalist[[i]]$actor)))
    #bind random order to df
    loop.datalist[[i]] <- mutate(loop.datalist[[i]], 
                                 xorderx = xorder[[i]]$xorder)
    #put randomized order of numbers in order to randomize rows in the dataset
    loop.datalist[[i]] <-
      loop.datalist[[i]][order(loop.datalist[[i]]$xorderx),]
    #bind time back on
    loop.data.randomized.time.added[[i]] <- bind_cols(loop.datalist[[i]], full_time_list[[i]])
    loop.data.randomized.time.added[[i]] <- subset(loop.data.randomized.time.added[[i]], select = -c(xorderx))

   }

  
#separate data into list of each obs session each day for each bird
actorer <- actor
actex <- c(1:length(actor))
dater <- unique(obs)
list_split_by_bird <- c()

for(i in datex2) {
for (g in actex) {output <- subset(loop.data.randomized.time.added[[i]], actor == actorer[g] |  subject == actorer[g])
#if one of the dfs in the list is empty, add NA
   if (nrow(output) < 1) {output [ nrow(output) + 1 , ] <- NA
    } 
  output <- mutate(output, counter = actorer[g])
  list_split_by_bird[[paste0("list", dater[i], actorer[g])]] <- output
}}

 
  #arrange df by time and date
list_split_by_bird <- lapply(list_split_by_bird, function(x) arrange(x, date, full_time))

  

  
  #rename data
 opp_aggression_list_2022G1 <- list_split_by_bird

  
  #modified each data frame to put consecutive events side by side
  opp_side_by_side <- lapply(opp_aggression_list_2022G1, function(x) mutate(x, actor_2 = lead(actor),actor_2_rank = lead(actor_rank),  subject_2 = lead(subject), subject_2_rank = lead(subject_rank),  full_time_2 = lead(full_time), date2 = lead(date)))
  
  
  
  
  
#counter identifies the focal individual for each dataframe after the
#master interaction dataframe is split into individual level dfs.
#Each df contains all events involving that individual (in any role).
#Using counter ensures that sequential behavioral sequences are evaluated from the
#correct individual’s perspective, reducing false positives that could
#occur if a pattern appears in one df but is actually explained by
#interactions only visible in another individual’s df.

#For example in individual A’s df we might see A attack B, then B attacks A, which appears to be retaliation. However, A’s df only includes interactions involving A and cannot show
# whether B interacted with others in between the initiating event and the aggressive response.
#If we look at B’s df (counter == B), we might see that the full sequence is A defeats B, then B defeats C, then B defeats A. Because B interacted with C before responding to A, the sequence was
#interrupted and is not counted as the response motif for retaliation. This interruption is invisible in A’s df and would create a false positive, which is why rules must be evaluated in the correct
# counter (focal individual) dataframe.

# In this loop initiated marks whether the focal individual was the subject. rule_followed indicates that the behavioral and time constraint patterns associated with a certain rule's response motif were followed and that the focal individual (counter) is correctly involved
  opp_side_by_side_tested <- map(opp_side_by_side, ~ mutate(.x, initiated = ifelse(
      subject == counter
      & subject_2 == counter
      & actor != actor_2
      & subject == subject_2
      & subject_2_rank < actor_2_rank, "yes","no"), 
    rule_followed = ifelse( 
      subject_2_rank < actor_2_rank 
      & subject == counter
      & subject_2 == subject
      & actor != actor_2
      & subject_2 == counter
      & full_time_2 - full_time <= 2444
      & full_time_2 - full_time > 0
      & date2 == date, "yes","no")))
  #combine dataframes into one huge dataframe
  opp_huge <- bind_rows(opp_side_by_side_tested, .id = "column_label")
  
  #sort by time and date
  opp_huge_sorted <- opp_huge %>%
    arrange(opp_huge$date, opp_huge$full_time)
  
  
  
  #Remove duplicate events, events that have been represented twice, and events that can not be confidently counted as the start of the oppalong response motif
  opp_huge_sorted_no_dupr <- subset(opp_huge_sorted, initiated != "no") 
  
  opp_no_dupr <- subset(opp_huge_sorted_no_dupr, select = c(date, full_time, actor, actor_rank, subject, subject_rank, 
                                                            counter, actor_2, actor_2_rank, subject_2, subject_2_rank, full_time_2, date2, 
                                                            initiated, rule_followed))
  
  
  #bind vector of run IDs for each run to the dataframe so that we have a numeric run id next to each data point that corresponds with that run which will allow us to sort in ascending order
  runIDs <- rep(loop.run, length(opp_no_dupr$actor))
  
  opp_complete <- cbind(runIDs, opp_no_dupr)
#put each runs data into the masterframe
  masterframe_opp.df  <- rbind(masterframe_opp.df, opp_complete)
}


#######################################################LOOP#END#######################################################



#write.csv(masterframe_opp.df,  here("ch1 data","opp2022G1countmaster_global_75perc.csv"), row.names = FALSE)

```

#Compare observed response motif exeuction to reference model
```{r}

#masterframe_opp.df <- read_csv(here("ch1 data","opp2022G1countmaster_global_75perc.csv"))


#put run #s in an ascending vector
cum_run_IDS <- unique(masterframe_opp.df$runIDs)
#create empty vector for number of time the response motif was executed to be stored
random_times_opp_followed <- c()


#put the number of times the response motif was executed into the vector for analysis 
for (j in cum_run_IDS) {
  len_of_opp <- length(which(masterframe_opp.df$rule_followed == "yes" & 
                                      masterframe_opp.df$runIDs == j))
  
  random_times_opp_followed <- c(random_times_opp_followed, len_of_opp)
}

#turn vector into a dataframe for analysis
rand_opp_.frame <- data.frame(random_times_opp_followed)

#plot observed response motif execution vs random 
 opp_2022G1_count_plot <-ggplot(rand_opp_.frame, aes(x=random_times_opp_followed)) + 
  geom_histogram(aes(y=..density..),      
                 binwidth=6,
                 colour="black", fill="grey") +
  geom_density() + 
  geom_vline(xintercept = opp_real_2022G1, colour = "red", size = 2) + geom_density(lwd = 1, linetype = 1) + theme_minimal() + theme(axis.text = element_text(size=25), axis.title= element_blank())+
  scale_x_continuous(name = "Count of Opportunism events", breaks=seq(0, 400, 50)) +  
     coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 400), ylim = c(0,.050)) +
scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(0, .05, .01)) 
 
opp_2022G1_count_plot


#calculate p value (proportion of randomized reference values that are more "extreme" than the observed count of rule response motif executions)
opp_tally2022G1 <-length(which(rand_opp_.frame$random_times_opp_followed > opp_real_2022G1))

finalopp_tally2022G1 <- 1 - opp_tally2022G1/length(rand_opp_.frame$random_times_opp_followed)
finalopp_tally2022G1
```

#Individual motif execution
```{r}

#test for individual execution of the motif associated with the rule. How many times did each bird execute a particular motif

unique(opp_doner_all_yes$actor_2)
length(unique(opp_doner_all_yes$actor_2)
)

summary.opp.individ_2022G1 <- opp_doner_all_yes %>% group_by(actor_2) %>%
  dplyr::summarize (
    count =n())

summary.opp.individ_2022G1







#compare to raw dataset to see if any birds didnt execute the motif at all
unique(t2022_aggression_only_readyG1$actor)
unique(t2022_aggression_only_readyG1$subject)




#for each rule keep in mind how many birds can actually execute the response motif
#Here the top ranked bird cant do opportunism
opp_nofollow <- setdiff(unique(t2022_aggression_only_readyG1$actor), unique(opp_doner_all_yes$actor_2)
)
opp_nofollow



#add in 0s for individuals who did not execute the motif for plotting purposes
opp_zeros <- rep(c(0),times=c(length(opp_nofollow)))


opp_nofollow_zeros<- data.frame(opp_nofollow,opp_zeros) 

colnames(opp_nofollow_zeros) <- c("actor_2","count")

summary.opp.individ_2022G1 <- rbind(summary.opp.individ_2022G1, opp_nofollow_zeros)

# Plot
individ_opp_plot_2022G1 <- ggplot(summary.opp.individ_2022G1, aes(x = actor_2, y = count )) + 
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", width = 0.6) + 
scale_y_continuous( breaks = seq(0, 60, 2)) + 
  theme_minimal()  +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90, vjust=0.5),
    axis.title.x = element_text( face = "bold",  margin = margin(t = 15)), 
    axis.title.y = element_text( face = "bold",  margin = margin(r = 15)),
    ) +
  labs(
    x = "Parakeet ID",  
    y = "Count of rule follows"   
  )

individ_opp_plot_2022G1
```
